title: Claude Code Configuration
description: Generate CLAUDE.md instructions, project settings, and MCP configuration for Claude Code.

steps.title: Claude Code Instructions (CLAUDE.md)
steps.description: Configure CLAUDE.md — the primary project instruction file treated as project context by Claude Code. Use CLAUDE.md for stable, durable guidance and invariants; avoid placing transient, task-specific workflow instructions here unless you explicitly want them included.
steps.hint: CLAUDE.md is treated as project context by Claude Code and is available to sessions. Keep it concise and focused on durable guidance; put reusable operational workflows into skills instead of embedding ephemeral instructions.

presets.label: Project Guidance
presets.value: Claude Code Project Guidance

presets.label: Engineering Standards
presets.value: Engineering Standards & AI Guidance

presets.label: Developer Handbook
presets.value: Developer Handbook — Claude Code

fields.label: Project Maturity
fields.description: Indicates whether this is a new, established, or mature codebase. This helps the generator choose defaults for testing strictness, refactor tolerance, and dependency policy.

fields.label: Stable project guidance
fields.description: Core, long-lived project rules and expectations that should remain consistent across tasks.
fields.placeholder: Add stable guidance that applies across the project, such as architecture principles, naming conventions, and team-wide expectations.
fields.screen_hint: Keep this field focused on durable guidance. Ephemeral workflow prompts belong in skills or task-specific hints.

fields.label: Task workflow hints
fields.description: Optional task-focused guidance. This field is not included in CLAUDE.md by default; consider moving durable workflows into skills.
fields.placeholder: Document workflow reminders that are useful for specific tasks, such as using planning mode for larger changes or how to prioritise fixes.
fields.screen_hint: These hints are optional render-time content and are not part of the core CLAUDE.md guidance unless explicitly selected.

fields.label: Project Overview
fields.description: A brief description of what this project does and its primary goals.
fields.placeholder: This project is a full-stack web application that...
fields.screen_hint: Start with a concise project overview so Claude makes context-aware decisions instead of guessing. Describe what the project does, who it serves, key goals, current development stage, and maturity. Click a preset for quick templates aligned to common project types.

presets.label: Greenfield project
presets.description: Starting a new project from scratch
presets.value: This is a new project starting from a clean foundation. The primary goals are to establish clean architecture, comprehensive test coverage, and automated CI/CD from day one. Code quality and maintainability take priority over speed of delivery.

presets.label: Existing codebase
presets.value: This is an established codebase with active development. Priority is maintaining consistency with existing patterns, avoiding regressions, and incrementally improving code quality. All changes must respect the existing architecture and conventions.

presets.label: API service
presets.value: This project is a backend API service exposing RESTful endpoints. It handles authentication, authorisation, input validation, and integrates with external services and data stores. Reliability, security, and clear API contracts are the top priorities.

presets.label: Full-stack application
presets.value: This is a full-stack web application with a frontend UI and backend API in a single repository. Changes often span both layers and should be tested end-to-end. Consistent patterns across the stack and clear separation of concerns are essential.

presets.label: CLI / Library
presets.value: This project is a command-line tool or reusable library distributed as a package. Public API stability, backwards compatibility, semantic versioning, and clear documentation are critical. Every breaking change requires a migration path.

presets.label: Monorepo / Multi-package
presets.description: Multiple packages in a single repository
presets.value: This is a monorepo containing multiple packages or services. Each package has its own build and test cycle. Cross-package changes must be coordinated and tested together. Shared code lives in common packages with clear ownership boundaries.

fields.label: Technology Stack
fields.description: Key technologies, frameworks, and libraries used across the project.
fields.placeholder:
- Frontend: React 19 + Vite + TypeScript + Tailwind CSS v4
- Backend: Python 3.13 + FastAPI + Pydantic v2
- Database: PostgreSQL with SQLAlchemy
fields.screen_hint: Let Claude know your full stack so it recommends patterns and libraries that already fit your project. See the presets for common stacks (React, FastAPI, Next.js, Go, Rust, Rails, etc.) or add your custom combination.

presets.label: React + Vite + TypeScript
presets.value:
- Frontend: React 19 + Vite + TypeScript
- Styling: Tailwind CSS v4
- State: React hooks — no Redux or Zustand

presets.label: FastAPI + Pydantic
presets.value:
- Backend: Python 3.13 + FastAPI + Pydantic v2
- ORM: SQLAlchemy (async)
- Python version: 3.13

presets.label: Next.js full-stack
presets.value:
- Framework: Next.js 15 App Router
- Language: TypeScript strict mode
- Styling: Tailwind CSS
- DB: Prisma + PostgreSQL

presets.label: Django + React
presets.value:
- Backend: Django 5 + Django REST Framework
- Frontend: React 19 + TypeScript
- Auth: Django sessions + JWT

presets.label: Go + HTTP
presets.value:
- Backend: Go 1.22+ with net/http or Chi router
- Testing: Go standard testing + testify
- Build: Go modules

presets.label: Spring Boot + Java/Kotlin
presets.value:
- Backend: Spring Boot 3 + Java 21 (or Kotlin)
- Build: Gradle (or Maven)
- Testing: JUnit 5 + Mockito

presets.label: .NET / C#
presets.value:
- Backend: .NET 8 + ASP.NET Core
- Language: C# 12
- Testing: xUnit + Moq

presets.label: Rust + Axum
presets.value:
- Backend: Rust + Axum (or Actix Web)
- Build: Cargo
- Testing: cargo test + proptest

presets.label: Ruby on Rails
presets.value:
- Framework: Ruby on Rails 7
- Language: Ruby 3.3
- Testing: RSpec + FactoryBot
- DB: PostgreSQL with ActiveRecord

presets.label: Vue + Node.js
presets.value:
- Frontend: Vue 3 + Vite + TypeScript
- Backend: Node.js + Express (or Fastify)
- Testing: Vitest + Supertest

fields.label: Coding Conventions
fields.description: Style rules, naming conventions, and patterns Claude should consistently follow.
fields.screen_hint: A locked baseline of core conventions is always included. Presets with mode "replace" adjust only the editable portion of this field and do not remove the locked guidance.
fields.locked_value:
- Prefer existing patterns over introducing new abstractions — consistency over novelty.
- Keep changes small and targeted to the stated task. Do not refactor code that was not part of the request.
- Each function or module should have a single clear responsibility.
- Use descriptive, intention-revealing names for variables, functions, and types.
- Prefer explicit types and clear interfaces at public boundaries.

presets.label: Robust architecture
presets.value:
- Prefer small, cohesive modules with a single responsibility.
- Keep abstractions simple and avoid overengineering.
- Use clear, intention-revealing names for functions, classes, and types.

presets.label: DRY & single responsibility
presets.value:
- Do not repeat logic — extract shared behaviour into well-named functions.
- Each function, class, or module should have a single clear responsibility.

presets.label: Descriptive naming
presets.value:
- Use descriptive, intention-revealing names for variables, functions, and types.
- Avoid abbreviations unless universally understood (e.g., id, url, http).
- Boolean variables and functions should read as questions: isValid, hasPermission, canRetry.

presets.label: Guard clauses
presets.value:
- Prefer early returns (guard clauses) over deeply nested conditionals.
- Handle error cases first, then proceed with the happy path.

presets.label: No magic values
presets.value:
- Extract magic numbers and hardcoded strings into named constants.
- Configuration values should come from environment variables or config files, not source code.

presets.label: Fail fast & loud
presets.value:
- Fail fast on invalid state — throw or return errors immediately rather than propagating bad data.
- Never silently ignore errors. Log or propagate every failure.

presets.label: Immutability first
presets.value: - Prefer immutability. Use const and readonly; avoid in-place mutation where possible.

presets.label: Error handling
presets.value:
- Handle errors explicitly. Never silently swallow exceptions.
- Use structured error types with clear messages. Include enough context to diagnose the failure.

presets.label: Small focused changes
presets.value:
- Keep changes small and focused — one concern per PR.
- Avoid mixing refactoring with feature work in the same commit.

presets.label: Prefer existing patterns
presets.value:
- Prefer existing patterns over introducing new abstractions.
- When in doubt, follow the convention already established in the codebase.

presets.label: TypeScript strict mode
presets.value: - Use TypeScript strict mode. Prefer explicit types at all public API boundaries.

presets.label: No `any` types
presets.value: - Never use `any` — use `unknown` and narrow with type guards.

presets.label: Named exports only
presets.value: - Use named exports exclusively. Avoid default exports.

presets.label: Async/await only
presets.value: - Use async/await throughout. Never use .then() chains or callbacks.

presets.label: Defensive boundaries
presets.value:
- Treat every external boundary (HTTP handlers, CLI args, file I/O, message queues) as untrusted.
- Parse and validate incoming data into typed structures before passing it deeper into the codebase.

presets.label: No secrets in code
presets.value:
- Never hardcode credentials, API keys, or secrets in source code.
- Load all sensitive values from environment variables or a secrets manager.

fields.label: Development Workflow
fields.description: Process steps Claude should follow when making changes. For tasks touching 3+ files or unfamiliar code, consider using Plan mode first to help scope the work and identify dependencies.
fields.screen_hint: These best-practice guardrails are always applied. Use this field to add project-specific workflow expectations on top of them. Reference how your team writes commits, where you store config, and what code review looks like. Click presets to load common workflows.
fields.locked_value:
- Read and understand the existing code before making changes.
- Run lint and tests before finalising any change. Fix all failures — never skip or disable tests to make a change pass.
- Review every change for security implications: validate inputs, check authentication, and ensure no secrets enter source control.
- Keep changes minimal and targeted to the stated task. Do not introduce unrequested features or refactors.
- Write clear commit messages that explain why, not just what.

presets.label: Test-driven
presets.value:
- Run lint and tests before finalising any change.
- Write or update tests for every new behaviour.
- Fix all test failures before marking a task complete.
- Never skip or disable existing tests to make a change pass.

presets.label: Minimal changes
presets.value:
- Keep changes minimal and targeted to the stated task.
- Do not refactor code that was not part of the request.
- Prefer small, well-described commits.
- Do not add features, dependencies, or abstractions beyond what was asked.

presets.label: Security-first
presets.value:
- Review every change for security implications before committing.
- Never introduce credentials, secrets, or tokens into source code.
- Validate that new endpoints have proper authentication and authorisation.
- Run dependency vulnerability checks before adding new packages.
- Reject any change that weakens existing security controls.

presets.label: CI/CD aligned
presets.value:
- Ensure every change passes the full CI pipeline locally before pushing.
- Run lint, type checks, and tests in the same order as CI.
- Do not merge code that has any failing checks.
- Keep the build green — fix broken builds immediately.

presets.label: Review-ready changes
presets.value:
- Write clear, descriptive commit messages explaining why, not just what.
- Keep each commit atomic — one logical change per commit.
- Ensure the codebase compiles and all tests pass after every commit.
- Include tests for all new behaviour and bug fixes.
- Self-review the diff before finalising.

presets.label: Documentation-aware
presets.description: Keep docs in sync with code
presets.value:
- Update relevant documentation when changing public APIs or behaviour.
- Add inline comments only when the why is not obvious from the code.
- Keep README and CONTRIBUTING guides up to date with actual commands.
- Ensure all public functions have clear doc comments.

presets.label: Comprehensive (recommended)
presets.value:
- Read the existing code and tests before making changes.
- Run lint and tests before finalising any change.
- Write or update tests for every new behaviour. Never skip or disable existing tests.
- Review every change for security implications — validate inputs, check auth, avoid secrets in code.
- Keep changes minimal and targeted to the stated task.
- Write clear commit messages explaining why, not just what.
- Update documentation when changing public APIs or behaviour.

fields.label: Additional Guidance
fields.description: Any other instructions, constraints, or context for Claude.
fields.screen_hint: Use this field to describe repo boundaries, protected areas, and team conventions. Avoid repeating the same security baseline from project settings.
fields.locked_value:
- Do not modify generated, vendored, or third-party files unless the task explicitly requires it.
- Do not edit lock files (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, Pipfile.lock, go.sum) by hand.
- Treat generated directories, vendor code, and deployment manifests as protected areas.
- Keep repository boundaries clear: changes should only touch the intended module or service.

presets.label: Security essentials
presets.description: Core security rules for any project
presets.value:
- Never commit secrets, API keys, tokens, or credentials to source control.
- Validate and sanitise all external input at system boundaries.
- Use parameterised queries — never interpolate user input into SQL or shell commands.
- Follow the principle of least privilege for all access controls.
- Escape output appropriately for the context (HTML, SQL, shell).

presets.label: No generated files
presets.value:
- Do not modify generated, vendored, or third-party files.
- Do not edit lock files (package-lock.json, yarn.lock, Pipfile.lock, go.sum) by hand.

presets.label: Dependency discipline
presets.value:
- Do not introduce new dependencies without explicit approval.
- Prefer well-maintained libraries with active security patching.
- Pin dependency versions for reproducible builds.
- Check for known vulnerabilities before adding any package.

presets.label: Reproducible builds
presets.value:
- All builds must be reproducible from a clean checkout.
- Use lock files and pinned versions for all dependencies.
- Document every environment variable required to build and run the project.
- Avoid relying on globally installed tools — use project-local versions.

presets.label: Logging & observability
presets.value:
- Use structured logging (JSON format) — never print raw strings for log output.
- Never log sensitive data: passwords, tokens, PII, or full request bodies.
- Include correlation IDs in all log entries for traceability.
- Use appropriate log levels: error for failures, warn for recoverable issues, info for key events.

presets.label: Error boundaries
presets.value:
- Every external call (HTTP, database, file I/O) must have error handling and reasonable timeouts.
- Return meaningful error messages to callers — never expose stack traces or internal paths to end users.
- Use retry logic with exponential backoff for transient failures.

presets.label: Backwards compatibility
presets.value:
- Do not introduce breaking changes to public APIs without a migration path.
- Deprecate before removing — mark old interfaces as deprecated for at least one release cycle.
- Version all API endpoints. Never modify the behaviour of an existing version.

presets.label: Testing standards
presets.value:
- Every bug fix must include a regression test that would have caught the bug.
- Tests must be deterministic — no flaky tests, no reliance on external services.
- Use descriptive test names that explain the expected behaviour.
- Test edge cases: empty inputs, boundary values, error conditions, and concurrent access.

steps.title: Build & Test Commands
steps.description: Provide machine-readable commands for the project's install, development, build, test, lint, typecheck, format check, and CI workflows.
steps.hint: Structured command fields make these inputs easier to render consistently for CLAUDE.md, project settings, or automation tooling.

steps.title: Accelerator Verification Policy
steps.description: Accelerator-maintained verification policy describing model, escalation, coverage and required checks. This is an accelerator abstraction and not a native Claude Code config file.
steps.hint: This file is maintained by the accelerator; it is not a documented Claude Code settings surface. Use it as the source-of-truth for CI/verification automation.

fields.label: Default evaluation model
fields.description: The default Claude model to use for evaluation and verification tasks.

validation.note: Model names are accelerator options and may not reflect the latest Claude Code releases. Consider validating dynamically or updating presets periodically.

fields.label: Escalation conditions
fields.description: When to switch to a higher-capability model or specialised agent.
fields.placeholder: E.g. escalate on ambiguous requirements, risky refactors, security issues, or cross-service changes.

fields.label: Coverage target
fields.description: A verification goal for test or review coverage, such as 90% or all touched modules.
fields.placeholder: 90% coverage or all touched files reviewed

fields.label: Required checks
fields.description: The list of checks or tools that must run before a change is considered verified.
fields.placeholder: List required verification steps such as lint, unit tests, security scan, type check.

steps.title: Skill Definitions (.claude/skills/)
steps.description: Define reusable skills. Each skill becomes a SKILL.md under .claude/skills/.
steps.hint: Skills are the recommended home for reusable operational workflows and automations.

fields.label: Skills
fields.description: One SKILL.md per entry. Provide structured metadata plus the SKILL.md content.

fields.label: Supporting files (JSON list)
fields.description: Optional supporting files as a JSON array of { path, content } objects.

steps.title: Project Settings (.claude/settings.json)
steps.description: Configure .claude/settings.json — project-level settings for Claude Code, committed for your whole team.
steps.hint: Claude Code merges settings in priority order: Managed > CLI args > Local > Project > User. Commit .claude/settings.json to set team defaults. Create .claude/settings.local.json (auto-gitignored) for personal overrides. The secure baseline (deny .env, secrets, keys) is always enforced.

fields.label: Settings JSON
fields.description: Edit the JSON for .claude/settings.json. The secure baseline above is always written to the file — your edits are merged on top.
fields.screen_hint: The secure baseline (deny access to .env, secrets, .pem keys) is always included and cannot be removed. Use this field to grant additional permissions (e.g., allow Docker access, allow writing to specific directories) or set project environment variables. Leave blank if the secure baseline is sufficient.

fields.label: Strict MCP config (runtime only)
fields.description: CLI/runtime policy: when enabled the launcher will invoke Claude Code with --strict-mcp-config to restrict MCP servers to the project-provided list. This is not written into settings.json.

presets.label: Secure baseline
Hardened security
presets.description: Block all secrets, keys, and sensitive configs

presets.label: Audit-friendly
Read-only mode
presets.description: Claude can read but not write files

presets.label: Enable Grading Tools
presets.description: Allow Claude to run grading and evaluation tools

presets.label: Repository boundaries
presets.description: Protect generated, vendor, and deployment files at the team level.

presets.label: Windows compatibility
presets.description: Add Windows-friendly path and shell handling for mixed environments.

steps.title: Project Hooks (.claude/hooks/)
steps.description: Define automation hooks for repository events and edit-time actions. Hooks are used for tasks such as formatting after edits, audit checks, notifications, and reinforcement of context.
steps.hint: Hooks are emitted under .claude/hooks/ as JSON or executable scripts depending on the hook type. These are accelerator-emitted artifacts that the project or CI can consume.

fields.label: Hooks
fields.description: One entry per hook. Provide a name, trigger, and the hook body or JSON configuration.

steps.title: Project MCP Configuration (.mcp.json)
steps.description: Configure project-scoped MCP servers shared with your team via version control.
steps.hint: Commit .mcp.json to share MCP servers with your team. MCP servers extend Claude Code with external tools: GitHub (PRs/issues), databases, error monitoring, web search, etc. Scope options: --scope local (current project), --scope project (team via git), --scope user (your account, all projects). Within Claude sessions, use /mcp to check status and /mcp auth SERVERNAME to authenticate OAuth. Avoid hardcoding secrets—use environment variables.

fields.label: MCP Configuration JSON
fields.description: Edit the JSON for .mcp.json. Click a server suggestion below to add it — existing servers are preserved.
fields.screen_hint: Each server listed here is available to Claude Code as a tool. Click any suggestion to add a server — it merges into mcpServers without overwriting existing entries.

presets.label: Playwright
presets.platform_notes: Commonly referenced in MCP documentation for browser automation.

presets.label: GitHub
presets.platform_notes: Commonly used for GitHub integration; may require local or OAuth authentication.

presets.label: Filesystem
presets.platform_notes: Useful for controlled repo access; ensure this is enabled only where filesystem operations are safe.

presets.label: Fetch
presets.description: HTTP fetch for web content and APIs
presets.platform_notes: Standard fetch server for retrieving remote content and API responses.

presets.label: Brave Search
presets.description: Web search via Brave Search API
presets.platform_notes: Community example for Brave Search integration; verify package availability before use.

presets.label: PostgreSQL
presets.description: Direct database queries and schema inspection
presets.platform_notes: Commonly used for database introspection; verify access permissions and URL format.

presets.label: Sentry
presets.platform_notes: Community-facing example; validate the package name and auth setup before use.

presets.label: Context7
presets.platform_notes: Community example for documentation lookup; confirm the package exists before use.

presets.label: Linear
presets.platform_notes: Community example for issue tracking. Validate package availability before use.

presets.label: Slack
presets.platform_notes: Community example for Slack access; verify the server package and auth model.

presets.label: Code Grader
presets.description: Automated code quality assessment and grading
presets.platform_notes: Custom/community example; package names should be validated before enabling.

presets.label: ESLint with Grading
presets.platform_notes: Community example for lint grading; verify the package and CLI options before use.

presets.label: Pylint Grader
presets.description: Python code analysis with grading scores
presets.platform_notes: Community example for Python lint grading; package availability should be verified.

steps.title: Subagent Definitions
steps.description: Define custom subagents that Claude Code can spawn to handle specialised tasks in your project.
steps.hint: Subagents are Claude Code sessions with specialized prompts and restricted tool sets, ideal for parallelizable work: testing, security review, documentation, refactoring. Use Sonnet for most agents; Opus for complex reasoning. Each generates one .md file in .claude/agents/. Give each agent a narrow focus—e.g., test-writer needs Read/Write/Bash; security-reviewer needs Read/Grep—this makes them faster and more reliable.

fields.label: Subagents
fields.description: Define one or more subagents. Each becomes a separate .md file inside .claude/agents/.
fields.screen_hint: Each subagent gets its own system prompt, tool set, and optionally a different model. Claude Code will automatically delegate to the right agent based on the task. Give each agent a focused responsibility — a narrow scope makes them more reliable.

presets.label: Security Reviewer
presets.description: Reviews code changes for security vulnerabilities

value.name: security-reviewer
value.description: Reviews code for security vulnerabilities and suggests fixes
value.system_prompt:
Review the provided code for security vulnerabilities including: SQL/command injection, broken authentication, insecure data handling, sensitive data exposure, missing authorisation checks, and leaked secrets or credentials.

For each issue found:
1. State the severity (Critical / High / Medium / Low)
2. Describe the vulnerability
3. Provide a concrete, minimal fix

Do not suggest stylistic changes — focus solely on security.

presets.label: Test Writer
presets.description: Writes unit and integration tests for changed code

value.name: test-writer
value.description: Writes comprehensive unit and integration tests for new or changed code
value.initial_prompt: Write tests for the provided code, using the existing project framework. Focus on happy paths, edge cases, and reproducible assertions.
value.system_prompt:
Write tests for the code you are given. Follow these rules:
- Mirror the file structure of the source (co-locate tests where the project convention dictates)
- Cover happy paths, edge cases, and error conditions
- Use the testing framework already present in the project
- Keep each test focused on one behaviour
- Run the tests after writing them and fix any failures before finishing

presets.label: Code Reviewer
presets.description: Reviews code for quality, maintainability and correctness

value.name: code-reviewer
value.description: Reviews code changes for correctness, maintainability, and adherence to project conventions
value.initial_prompt: Act as a code reviewer focused on correctness, maintainability, and adherence to existing project patterns.
value.system_prompt:
Review the provided code changes as a thorough senior engineer would in a pull request review.

Focus on:
- Correctness: logic errors, off-by-one errors, unhandled edge cases
- Maintainability: complex or unclear code that should be simplified
- Consistency with existing codebase patterns
- Missing error handling
- Performance issues that are obvious without profiling

Be constructive. For each issue, explain why it matters and suggest how to fix it.

presets.label: Documentation Writer
presets.description: Adds and updates inline docs and README content

value.name: documentation-writer
value.description: Writes and updates inline documentation, docstrings, and README content
value.system_prompt:
Write and update documentation for the provided code. Follow the documentation style already present in the project (JSDoc, Python docstrings, etc.).

For each public function, class, or module:
- Write a concise one-line summary
- Document all parameters and return values
- Include a usage example where it adds clarity
- Note any important side effects or exceptions

Do not modify source logic — only touch documentation.

value.name: refactoring-specialist
value.description: Safely refactors code to improve clarity and reduce duplication without changing behaviour
value.system_prompt:
Refactor the provided code to improve its structure while strictly preserving observable behaviour.

Priorities (in order):
1. Extract repeated logic into well-named functions
2. Simplify complex conditionals
3. Remove dead code
4. Apply the project's established patterns and naming conventions

Run the test suite after each significant change. Do not introduce new features or change external interfaces.

presets.label: Dependency Auditor
presets.description: Audits dependencies for vulnerabilities and licensing

value.name: dependency-auditor
value.description: Audits project dependencies for known vulnerabilities, outdated versions, and licensing concerns
value.system_prompt:
Audit the project's dependencies for security and compliance issues.

For each dependency manifest found (package.json, requirements.txt, go.mod, Cargo.toml, pom.xml, Gemfile, etc.):
1. Run the appropriate audit command (npm audit, pip-audit, go vuln check, cargo audit, etc.)
2. Identify dependencies with known CVEs — state severity and CVE ID
3. Flag outdated packages with available security patches
4. Note any deprecated or unmaintained packages
5. Check for overly permissive licences that may conflict with the project

Provide a summary table: total dependencies, vulnerabilities by severity, and prioritised remediation steps.

presets.label: Performance Analyst
presets.description: Identifies performance bottlenecks and suggests targeted fixes

value.name: performance-analyst
value.description: Analyses code for performance issues and suggests targeted optimisations
value.system_prompt:
Analyse the provided code for performance issues.

Focus on:
- N+1 query patterns in database access
- Unnecessary allocations or copies in hot paths
- Missing pagination on list endpoints
- Unbounded loops or recursive calls without depth limits
- Missing caching for expensive, idempotent operations
- Synchronous I/O blocking the event loop or main thread
- Missing connection pooling or resource reuse

For each issue:
1. Describe the bottleneck and its likely impact
2. Provide a concrete fix with a code example
3. Estimate the improvement (order of magnitude)

Do not suggest micro-optimisations. Focus on changes that will have measurable impact under production load.

presets.label: Migration Planner
presets.description: Plans safe, reversible database and API migrations

value.name: migration-planner
value.description: Plans safe, reversible database schema and API migrations
value.initial_prompt: Plan a migration safely without executing destructive operations automatically. Focus on rollback paths and compatibility.
value.system_prompt:
Plan and implement safe migrations for the project.

For each migration:
1. Analyse the current schema or API contract
2. Design the migration to be backwards-compatible where possible
3. Always include a rollback path — generate both forward and reverse migration scripts
4. Validate that existing data will not be lost or corrupted
5. Document any breaking changes and the steps to handle them
6. For database migrations, prefer additive changes (add column, add table) over destructive ones

Never run destructive operations (DROP, DELETE, TRUNCATE) automatically — list them and wait for confirmation.
